Can you post a transcript of the command line session that works, as well as a transcript of the gdbgui session and what the error is? If the only error is the message `File not found: main`, that's something I can fix in gdbgui. You can also turn this off by unchecking Settings > Add breakpoint to main after loading executable.

> IMO this is the first anomaly to investigate. Why does running the cross GDB directly produce a different result than running it under gdbgui?

In gdbgui are you connecting to the remote target or just loading the elf binary? It looks like in the commandline session you're running gdb, loading .elf symbols, then connecting to the remote target. But in gdbgui it looks like you are running gdbgui, loading .elf symbols, and then attempting to break at main. Is this correct?

The reason you're getting `/usr/bin/arm-none-eabi-gdb: unrecognized option '-exec-arguments'` is because gdb needs to be launched with the `--interpreter=mi2` flag to understand [machine interface](https://sourceware.org/gdb/onlinedocs/gdb/GDB_002fMI.html) commands, such as the ones gdbgui sends like `-exec-arguments`.

@isaguimiot OK, I put together a quick implementation today, which you can find here: https://github.com/Grunny/zap-cli/compare/context-and-user-scans

It was a quick PoC, so I want to clean it up, add some validation, add tests, etc. but did you want to give it a test and see if it meets your needs, and give any feedback on the parameters and so forth? :) You can install it in a virtualenv by cloning the repo, checking out the branch `context-and-user-scans`, and then installing it in a virtualenv with `pip install -e .`.

You can check out each of the `context` commands with `zap-cli context --help`, but the basic idea is that you can now import a saved context file, and then run the spider or scanner by providing the context name and user name, i.e.
```
zap-cli context import /path/to/context.context
zap-cli -v open-url "http://localhost/"
zap-cli -v spider --context-name DevTest --user-name GrunnyTest "http://localhost"
zap-cli -v active-scan -c DevTest -u GrunnyTest "http://localhost"
zap-cli -v quick-scan --spider -c DevTest -u GrunnyTest "http://localhost"
```

The ZAP API in general requires the context ID and user ID (both integers), but I figured using the plain text names was easier for users, so `zap-cli` gets the IDs based on the names when you provide them.

Let me know what you think. :)

The Unicode Consortium has agreed these emoji characters should be allowed 2 cells for display on terminals, and my software repeats this claim to yours. Unfortunately, the terminal emulator used does not agree. Commonly, such software is based upon older "wcwidth", the libc on your system is often woefully out of date.

I think you might notice if you play with this emulator you're displaying here, and set "blinking block cursor", that immediately following the input and echo output of a snake, your blinking cursor occludes the snake -- that is, the snake *should* be allowed 2 cells, but the emulator advanced only one.

Unless there is newer unicode release data that says the snake emoji may be 1 cell wide, there is no fix for me to make in wcwidth. This was a choice I made, not to chose against any particular emulator. As written,

"This library aims to be forward-looking, portable, and most correct."

and later caveats,

"This library attempts to determine the printable width by an unknown targeted terminal emulator. It does not provide any ability to discern what the target emulator software, version, of level of support is. Results may vary!"

I stress the *Results may vary*. xterm, iTerm2, rxvt-unicode, whatever windows is doing today with ubuntu, these all vary in level of support for wide unicode characters.

I'm afraid theres no clear resolution from this end.

Okay, I just did some work on this issue. Unfortunately it is somewhat impossible to obtain the precise type of a given generator object at runtime without evaluating it actively. However, if the underlying generator function is type-annotated, pytypes can figure out the type from that annotation, e.g.

```
def testfunc_Generator():
	# type: () -> Generator[int, Union[str, None], Any]
	s = yield
	while not s is None:
		if s == 'fail':
			s = yield 'bad yield'
		s = yield len(s)
```
Here ``deep_type(testfunc_Generator())`` should work as expected. I know e.g. pyannotate and monkeytype resolve this situation by looking at the bytecode, but that would not port easily to other python interpreters, e.g. to Jython. Nevertheless we can revisit that path sooner or later. Maybe typing-folks comes up with a proper solution...

For now, ``deep_type`` will plainly return ``Generator`` if no annotation can be found. This should ultimately solve the issue you described. Commit about to come...

I think it's going to require a bit more than adjusting the timeouts to fix the problem; I've tested a timeout of one second as a read timeout, and what happens is that the process will wake up after the read times out, wasting CPU cycles. I also ran into an issue during testing with a high latency connection where the read timeout ended up bubbling up after several failures, even though it wouldn't normally have done so.

Ultimately, I think that urllib3's handling of chunked streams is just too primitive for high performance use cases. If you're set on using timed reads as a solution, a call to `select` will at least work around the busy-wait issue mentioned above, but it doesn't fix everything. Really, why stop at a chunk size of 1024? A larger chunk size is always going to provide better performance provided that you have the memory for it.

@kynan, the python rewrite is very different internally - so PR won't be possible. It doesn't use any of the nbconvert/nbformat stuff. it's faster than rapidjson or jq. On the other hand it only does the stripping out (no installation tools, validation, etc.).

I first tried to get `nbstripout` to use `rapidjson` or `ujson`, but neither has an identical API to `json`. So that didn't work.

Given that most likely the strip out has to be as fast as possible for using git with many notebooks, i'd say the original `nbstripout` needs to be split into 2 pieces - one optimized for speed of the critical path of doing the strip out and another for installation/configuration/validation/etc. If you do that then you can just use `nbstripout-fast` as is - with a few tweaks to support keep_outputs and and a few other flags. And leave the rest of the features in the other script.

Also,  `nbstripout-fast` is already written in the whitelist format as you suggested [here](https://github.com/kynan/nbstripout/issues/85#issuecomment-412365346), except the fields are hardcoded and will probably need to be configurable to make it generic enough for anybody to use without changes to the source code.

And yes, feel free to incorporate `nbstripout-jq` in your repo. it too will need some tweaks to be identical to `nbstripout`. We dropped the jq version since pure python json rewrite `nbstripout-fast` is faster.

p.s. if you decide to integrate/adopt the code the -d option keeps outputs and also preserves a few other metadata fields that we use for documentation notebooks, which differs from code notebooks. Obviously this won't be useful for a general user.

Thanks for the stacktrace. I see the problem. We try to avoid deadlocks by releasing the client lock before processing callbacks (see `client._pending_completion`). But connection errors will trigger an immediate errback and do not release the client lock before processing. So if an errback needs the coordinator lock then we have a possibility of deadlock. And that is exactly what your stacktrace shows here.

I can think of two approaches to fixing this off the top of my head: (1) defer errback handling until after releasing the client lock, (2) release the coordinator lock in the heartbeat thread when calling client.poll.

from django.core.management.commands.runserver import Command
```
This would then shadow the `staticfiles` runserver command and replace it with the default one.

With ``$BROWSER``, RTV has different behavior depending on if you're going to open the browser in a new window (like firefox, chrome, etc.) or if it needs to yield control of the terminal to the application (like w3m, your script, etc.). I haven't found a foolproof way to determine this so for now it [makes an educated guess](https://github.com/michael-lazar/rtv/blob/master/rtv/terminal.py#L92) based on your environment. In your case, RTV is getting confused and you end up with two applications fighting for control of the terminal. You might be able to work around your issue by setting ``DISPLAY=`` to force RTV into terminal browser mode.

I think that ideally you would also be able to define this with a mailcap entry like so

``text/html; w3m http://justread.mpgarate.com/read?url=%s; needsterminal``

It looks like RTV doesn't respect ``text/html`` mailcap entries though, and defers to ``$BROWSER`` so you would likely end up with the same problem.

I solved this problem the easiest way. Inherit class with explicit support for subcommands. Without need of subparsers:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Extension of ConfigArgParse for better handling of subcommands
It is inherited and has only one change in api:
 - first param of constructor is list of subcommands
"""

__author__ = "Ondřej Profant"
__version__ = 0.1
__all__ = ["ByroParse"]

import sys
import configargparse


class ByroParse(configargparse.ArgParser):
    """
    Extension of ConfigArgParse for better handling of subcommands
    It is inherited and has only one change in api:
     - first param of constructor is list of subcommands
    """

    def __init__(self, commands, *args, **kwargs):
        self.commands = commands
        super(ByroParse, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)

    def parse_args(self):
        if len(sys.argv) < 2:
            self.print_help()
            sys.exit()

        cmd = sys.argv[1]
        args = sys.argv[2:]

        if cmd not in self.commands + ["-v", "--version"]:
            print(cmd)
            self.print_help()
            sys.exit()

        parsed = super(ByroParse, self).parse_args(args)

        parsed.command = cmd

        return parsed

if __name__ == "__main__":
    parser = ByroParse(
        ["foo", "bar"],
        default_config_files=["config.ini"],
        args_for_setting_config_path=["-c", "--config"]
    )

    # version is specific, it can be use without subcommands
    parser.add_argument("-v", "--version", action='version', version="%(prog)s 2.0")
    parser.add_argument("-f", "--force-level")
    parser.add_argument("-d", "--debug-level")
    # ...

    print(parser.parse_args())
```

Now treelib supports node attribute modification:

### Update non-root node
```python
from treelib import Tree, Node
tree = Tree()
tree.create_node("Harry", "harry")
tree.create_node("Jane", "jane", parent="harry")

tree.update_node("jane", identifier='xyz')
assert tree["xyz"].identifier == 'xyz'
```

### Update root node
```python
from treelib import Tree, Node
tree = Tree()
tree.create_node("Harry", "harry")
tree.create_node("Jane", "jane", parent="harry")

tree.update_node("harry", identifier='xyz')
assert tree.root == 'xyz'
assert tree['jane'].bpointer == 'xyz'
```

Well... this particular bit should be identical on linux and windows, and the tests pass on linux, so I'm fairly sure this particular bit is ok.  But, to answer your question about string length.... strings in C/C++ can take multiple forms.  One form is null-terminated, which comprises one byte for each character in the string, and then one additional byte, which is set to 0, which is used to indicate the end of the string.  The length of the string, as returned by eg `strlen` excludes that final null-byte.  The length of the array containing that string is one more than the `strlen` length of the string.

My guess is that I'm reading it and the Unicode characters are wider than I expect.  unixODBC always uses 2-byte UCS2, so I'll look at each character in 2-byte chunks. There is some very strange behavior here.  You'll notice that you send out one request in [1] but you get two replies in [2] and [3], but they are coming from two different Ethernet addresses?  I'm not familiar with the "Linux cooked capture" header, or the distinction between "Unicast to us (0)" and the "Sent by us(4)".

Next, your request in [4] is from 10.109.3.38 to 10.199.3.56, but the response [5] is going from 10.199.3.56 to 10.109.3.59, and then another response [6] is going from 10.199.3.56 to 10.109.3.38 as it should.

Your router-to-network command is telling the stack that 10.199.3.56 is the router to these other networks and the application is requesting some from a device on one of those networks, send it to this device as a router and it will forward it along.  This is analogous to sending an IP packet to the Ethernet address of a router (which is sadly called a default gateway) and the router figures out how to get it where it needs to go.

The "nice" thing about [13], [16], and [19] is that they were explicitly rejected with an error.  The not so nice thing is that the error that came back was from a different device than the request was sent to!  Notice how in [13] the destination network is 2454 and the address `0a:c7:03:38:ba:c0`, which matches the IP address you put in the console.  *However,* the source address in the response in [14] is also from network 2454, but the MAC address is `10`.  This is why BACpypes is timing out, the source and destination addresses don't line up.

Just for more information, could you please run the `samples\WhoIsRouter.py` application with debugging turned on:

    $ python samples/WhoIsRouter.py --debug
    > irt 10.199.3.56

This will send an `InitializeRoutingTable` request to the device and because we are not providing it with a new routing table, the response coming back will be its current routing table.  Then we can find out which networks like 2454 are on the other side of the router, and also see if the routing tables are scrambled.  At this point I think your read request should look like this:

    > rtn 10.199.3.56 2454
    > read 2454:10 analogValue 38 presentValue

You should immediately get back an error saying that the object doesn't exist (rather than timing out), then you have to figure out what objects are there, and I can help with that once we get some of this other cruft out of the way.

The issue seems to kick in because a file has been *closed* and although the snippet doesn't show how `execute` is actually been used, a sensible guess would be:

  - The same `cerebro` is being used over and over again for the backtesting

Be it the case: it is wrong! (If not some extra clues would be needed)

A `Cerebro` instance is only meant to be used once and reusing it would lead to problems like that one.

Like pointed out above and if that's the case, a sensible setup would create a new cerebro instance for each run.

Ideally, there should be no need to recreate the *data feeds*, but they would unfortunately go through the preloading phase again, because of the fact that *cerebro is only meant to be used once*.

The use case is anyhow interesting and there could be a potential setup which would avoid the data feeds being reloaded over and over again:

  - Optimization

This may seem strange because *optimization* is meant for the optimization of a single strategy and your case indicates that different strategies are actually being tested. This could mix both approaches:

  - Create a generic strategy with a specific parameter which is the key to fetching the real strategy to optimize from your database

  - Add this generic strategy with `optstrategy` and an iterable with the keys to fetch the databases

  - Run *cerebro* (optionally with `cerebro.run(optreturn=False)` if not only the analyzers are wished)

    You may also run with `cerebro.run(maxcpus=1)` to mimic the current behavior (or else all cores will be used)

In this scenario the datas will be preloaded once and shared across the different iterations of the optimization.

Let me know if this may help

P.S.
It may be sensible to look into adding support for a reusable cerebro,

I know this issue is now years old, but I'd like to throw a +1 into the ring.  With the current trend in APIs is microservices, there's a huge need arising which is the need to parallelize testing of those APIs.  Currently, with a traditional "setup, do test, tear-down" approach to testing microservice APIs for larger APIs can take a (relatively) long time.  PyRestTest helps in that regard and allows passing objects from one to the next to have simplified shared dependencies, but it (currently) runs tests in a non-concurrent fashion.  With a bit of effort, each test could be pre-loaded and evaluate what it's dependency is to generate a dependency graph, and the optimize by performing the tests with the most dependencies first, so that its dependents could fire upon its completion.  This would allow for full parallelization of the tests, and in the microservice world, could allow for a full barrage of tests to complete in seconds, not minutes/hours.

Imagine the velocity gained by this approach.  I've spent a few days googling for an API testing framework (or a testing framework in general) which can do this, and I haven't found any.  But, this framework comes awfully close to being able to do this by allowing artifacts/values from previous steps to cascade into the next tests, and by doing things like supporting saving outputs from steps, so that we can do a cleanup afterwards based on those outputs.

I guess I don't see a ton of movement on this project lately, but if it's still active and if anyone's interested, this might be something I would approach doing a PoC of with this framework since I really see the "future" in microservices will require test parallelization.

The wrapper implements ``__dir__()`` as:

```
    def __dir__(self):
        return dir(self.__wrapped__)
```

so introspection will only see what was on the original object.

If you need introspection to also show additional methods only present on the wrapper, you would need to override ``__dir__()`` to return a combination of what is present on the wrapped object and the wrapper.

Note that in doing this, there could be a risk if the behaviour of any code using the wrapped object via the wrappers would be changed by showing additional methods not on the wrapped object.

Can't this be done trivially with `All()` and a custom function?
```python
def passwords_must_match(passwords):
    if passwords['password'] != passwords['password_again']:
        raise Invalid('passwords must match')
    return passwords

s=Schema(All(
    {'password':str, 'password_again':str, 'password },
    passwords_must_match
))

s({'password':'123', 'password_again':'123'})

# raises MultipleInvalid: passwords must match
s({'password':'123', 'password_again':'and now for something completely different'})
```

This leads to a reasonably clean pattern where you use `All` to first validate the general structure of your document (by passing in a regular schema dict) and then move on to your more special rules that involve more than one field. 

Extending a little further to the password length-checking case:
```python
def password_min_length(passwords):
    if len(passwords['password']) < passwords['min_length']:
        raise Invalid('password should be at least {} characters'.format(passwords['min_length']))
    return passwords

s2=Schema(All(
    {'password':str, 'password_again':str, 'min_length':int},
    passwords_must_match,
    password_min_length
))

# MultipleInvalid: password should be at least 5 characters
s2({'password':'123', 'password_again':'123', 'min_length': 5})
```

Below are examples of the cases you're talking about. There's no difference between the standard requests behaviors and requests-futures other than things moving to the `results()` call. Your best bet is to look at the requests docs and questions/examples for it and move where the exception is thrown.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

from pprint import pprint
from requests_futures.sessions import FuturesSession
from requests.exceptions import ConnectTimeout, HTTPError, ReadTimeout

session = FuturesSession()

future = session.get('http://httpbin.org/get', timeout=0.01)
try:
    # This will throw a ConnectionTimeout exception b/c 0.01 isn't long enough
    # to connect
    resp = future.result()
except ConnectTimeout as e:
    pprint(e)

future = session.get('http://httpbin.org/delay/5', timeout=1)
try:
    # this will throw a ReadTimeout exception b/c 1s is enough to connect, but
    # less time than we've asked httpbin to delay things
    resp = future.result()
except ReadTimeout as e:
    pprint(e)


future = session.get('http://httpbin.org/status/502')
# This will not throw an exception, b/c requests doesn't do that for http
# status codes (http errors)
resp = future.result()
try:
    # This will throw an exception for 4xx and 5xx statues, it'd be a bit weird
    # to call it and immediately catch it as I'm doing here, it'd probably be
    # cleaner to just check the status code directly, if you want it to throw,
    # but don't want to catch it right here then it'd make sense.
    resp.raise_for_status()
except HTTPError as e:
    pprint(e)
```

@ilevkivskyi : The reason that `name` and `type` are in the function `field()` is to support dynamic class creation using `make_class()`:
```
C = make_class('C',
               [field('x', int),
                field_with_default_factory(callable, 'y', list),
                ])
```
being the same as:
```
@dataclass
class C:
    x: int
    y: list=field_with_default_factory(callable)
```
We could create another set of functions that are just like `field` and `field_with_default_factory` but without `name` and `type`, but then we'd have to name them and document the differences. As the code currently stands, it's an error to supply them when using them in "normal" class syntax. Although doing so would give us some better flexibility in the order and naming of parameters, especially in `field_with_default_factory`. I'm not wild about the order of (callable, name, type) when using `make_class()`, but it improves just specifying the callable better in normal class syntax (as in my second example above). If you insist on the one directory approach then I have a better alternative. Setting `PYTHONPATH` prepends the path to `sys.path`, so what you want is to *append* to it. To do this, create a `sitecustomize.py` module somewhere in the regular Python path, and add the code that appends your custom package directory to `sys.path`. This will prevent Python from loading the wrong `concurrent.futures` module.

I have a similar issue - my best theory is something around this: http://bugs.python.org/issue6721

If you have multiple threads (which you will do if you use multiprocessing, since there are queue feeder threads etc.) and one of these threads holds a lock when the process forks, the lock is never released on the child. In particular, this affects logging (but the multiprocessing has it's own logging which apparently is not affected), and also flushing stdout.

My code to exit to console belongs in a Frame which looks like:

    class TestRunnerView(Frame):
        def __init__(self, screen, model):
            super(TestRunnerView, self).__init__(
                                            screen,
                                            screen.height * 4 // 5,
                                            screen.width * 4 // 5,
                                            hover_focus=True,
                                            has_shadow = True,
                                            title="Running Test Scheme")

            # Setup the screen
            self._model = model
            self._screen = screen
            self.fix()

        def reset(self):
            # Do standard reset to clear out form, then populate with new data.
            super(TestRunnerView, self).reset()
            self._start()

        def _start(self):
            dut = self._model.get_current_device();
            self._screen.close()
            print("Starting...")
            self.runTestSchemeFromPartNumber(dut["serial_number"])
            raise(RestartApp("Finished running tests... Restarting application"))
            
Where ```RestartApp``` is a custom exception:

    class RestartApp(Exception):
        pass


This is caught by the app runner which builds the application:

    def start_app():
        devices = DeviceModel()
        last_scene = None

        while True:
            try:
                Screen.wrapper(testrunner, 
                               catch_interrupt=True, 
                               arguments=[last_scene, devices])
                sys.exit(0)
            except ResizeScreenError as e:
                last_scene = e.scene
            except RestartApp as r:
                print(r)

I could make an exception like the ResizeScreenError which passes which Scene to jump into but for now I'm quite happy with the app returning to its starting point.

I found a workaround however it requires changing the library code:

In the package change the file in this path: google/modules/utils.py

In the following method add another field `'cr': 'countryUS'` into the dictionary named as 'params':
This is for changing the location to United States, you can do the same according to your own preference. (e.g. for Turkey it is countryTR)
```
def _get_search_url(query, page=0, per_page=10, lang='en'):
    # note: num per page might not be supported by google anymore (because of
    # google instant)

    params = {'nl': lang, 'q': query.encode(
        'utf8'), 'start': page * per_page, 'num': per_page}

Ok, I think I've finally figured it out. 
How it worked:
     - First Django loads the model formset with predefined widgets for different
       field types. If there's a date based field, then it loads the _AdminDateWidget_
       and it's required media to context under `{{media.js}}` in _templates/admin/change_list.html_.
       (Note: it accumulates media in _django.forms.widgets.Media object_,
       which prevents duplicates, but the _DateRangeFilter_ is not included yet
       since it's not model field related.
       List of predefined widgets is in _django.contrib.admin.options.FORMFIELD_FOR_DBFIELD_DEFAULTS_)
     - After that Django starts rendering forms, which have the `{{form.media}}`
       tag. Only then the `DjangoRangeFilter.get_media()` is called and rendered,
       which creates the duplicates.
How it works:
     - first step is the same, if there's a _AdminDateWidget_ to be loaded then
       nothing changes
     - DOM gets rendered and if the _AdminDateWidget_ was rendered then
       the _DateTimeShortcuts.js_ is initiated which sets the `window.DateTimeShortcuts`.
       Otherwise, the `window.DateTimeShortcuts` is undefined.
     - The lines below check if the `DateTimeShortcuts` has been set and if not
       tthen the _DateTimeShortcuts.js_ and _calendar.js_ is rendered
     - if there's more than one _DateRangeFilter_ then the only the first {{form.media.js}} will return the static js files, all the subsequent requests will return []. This happens by setting a `DJANGO_RANGEFILTER_ADMIN_JS_SET` on request.

How it could work:
     - in `DateTimeShortcuts.js` the `init` method is attached to the eventListener on `loads`. It doesn't check if it has been done previously, that's why we get multiple identical objects listening on `loads`. So in `admin/js/admin/DateTimeShortcuts.js` instead of 
```javascript
    window.addEventListener('load', DateTimeShortcuts.init);
    window.DateTimeShortcuts = DateTimeShortcuts;
```
it should be 
```javascript
 if (window.DateTimeShortuts == 'undefined') {
    window.addEventListener('load', DateTimeShortcuts.init);
    window.DateTimeShortcuts = DateTimeShortcuts;
 }
```

I was unable to duplicate the problem in pydal, but on examination I think the problem is in ```gluon/validators.py```.  The stack trace attached above indicates:

```
  File "C:\Users\David\Google Drive\My Documents\ocsnedb50\gluon\validators.py", line 572, in build_set

    records = self.dbset(table).select(*fields, **dd)
```

Line 572 is in an 'if' block that uses the private attribute '_dbname' to determine if gae specific code should be run, the test evidently fails, because the non-gae code is being run:

```python
564: if self.dbset.db._dbname != 'gae':
    ...

572:     records = self.dbset(table).select(*fields, **dd)
573: else:
    ...
```

There appears to currently be 4 different google adapters:

``` python
    # discouraged, for backward compatibility
    ADAPTERS['gae'] = GoogleDatastoreAdapter
    # add gae adapters
    ADAPTERS['google:datastore'] = GoogleDatastoreAdapter
    ADAPTERS['google:datastore+ndb'] = GoogleDatastoreAdapter
    ADAPTERS['google:sql'] = GoogleSQLAdapter
```

So the test: ```.db._dbname != 'gae'```, will fail for three of the four google adapters. ```gluon/validators.py``` appears to have already solved a similar problem once with this test:

```python
613: from pydal.adapters import GoogleDatastoreAdapter
    ...

617: if GoogleDatastoreAdapter is not None and isinstance(self.dbset.db._adapter, GoogleDatastoreAdapter):
    ...
```

Though, that is still a somewhat clunky way to solve the problem.  If applications are going to need to know that they are using a GAE adapter, then there should likely be better support for determining that fact from PYDAL. ```

```

It does a little more than boto's `set_contents_from_filename` though.  It will automatically switch over to parallel multipart uploads if the file size is over a size threshold.

Temporary fix for my problem at https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-cli-py/issues/22 is `--no-binary boto3`.

The `Prefix` parameter is not for the filename but the string `Key` used to store the object in your bucket. To use your examples:
```json
{
"Bucket": "bucket001",
"Key": "FolderName/filename.db"
}
```
```json
{
"Bucket": "bucket002",
"Key": "Somefolder/filename.db"
}
```

There's two options:
1) You can restructure how you store objects in S3 so that a `Prefix` could filter for the files you're looking for.
2) List all objects in the bucket and see if the `Key` ends with the filename you're looking for.

I just spent some time to find something along the lines of calling `release()` on the `odb` instance of the repository, but only came to the conclusion that such functionality does not exist.
When I wrote GitPython for py2.X, I was counting on the somewhat deterministic destruction of objects, and built everything around that. However, by now this is simply not the case anymore (if it ever was ...), so GitPython does have a problem with releasing system resources properly in some cases.

A known workaround for this issue is to fork code into it's own subprocess, to allow it to be cleaned up by the operating system when done. Doing this in your case might add a lot of complexity.

Something you could try is to use libgit2 directly, which will by it's very nature provide methods to release resources explicitly.

Also I am afraid there no fix for this issue at this time, unless someone is willing to dig in and assure respective release methods are added to the types in question.

@Midnighter you can't set your `WORKDIR` to `app/` and then `COPY Pipfile* app/` -- this moves you into `/app` and then copies your `Pipfile` into `/app/app` which means `pipenv` doesn't find a Pipfile to work with.  I simply did this and it worked:

```dockerfile
FROM python:3.6-alpine3.7

ENV PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1


COPY Pipfile* app/

WORKDIR app/

RUN set -x \
    && pip install --upgrade pip setuptools wheel pipenv \
    && pipenv install --system --dev
```

The idea with `StubTreq` is that your HTTP route can return a `Deferred`, but the idea is that it is talking to a deterministic test mock *so that you can cause that `Deferred` to be synchronously fired at an exact point in the test of your choosing*.  If the `Deferred` isn't fired yet, then it does you no good to call `flush`, and if the `Deferred` is waiting upon a real thread to execute, it will fire on its own schedule, not yours.  Note in the example that you gave the line that does `rsrc.stored_request.finish`; that is causing the server to actually respond to the request.

In the case that you're describing, the test is fundamentally broken, because since the response is async, `treq.request` is never going to finish until `treq.flush` is called, and `treq.flush` only gets called *after* the `yield treq.request` expression completes… and it never will.

Hopefully you're not actually trying to test a trivial `deferToThread` call that returns a dictionary, since that would be a bit silly, so you probably want to ask about what you're actually trying to do rather than an example that doesn't do anything useful :).

I hope that I've answered your question, but this is a bug tracker, not a discussion forum, and this doesn't describe a bug in Klein, so I'm closing the issue.  If you want to ask questions like this, in the future, either Stack Overflow or https://twistedmatrix.com/cgi-bin/mailman/listinfo/twisted-python/ is the place to do it.

Thanks for using Klein, Twisted, & Treq!  Sorry that you're having a hard time getting the hand of testing.  I hope to hear from you on the mailing list.

@tigran-a The pykafka consumer and producer don't currently support maintaining their connections when the cluster has less nodes available than the replication factor dictates. This isn't trivial, since we need the user to be aware that they're not consuming or producing to all partitions. This may be something we'll be able to address in a future release, but I'm still going over the documentation to try and figure out exactly how/if such cases are supposed to be handled by clients.

Turns out that this is correct and we should clear up the name differences between the "Offset API" and the "Offset Commit/Fetch API". We need one term for committing and fetching actual offsets - for this, I think `commit_offset` and `fetch_offset` make sense. We also need a term for fetching earliest and latest offsets - perhaps `fetch_offset_range` and `fetch_offset_bound` make sense here.

@rogaha Thanks very much for this. It's definitely addressing something that's been a problem for quite a while now - the fact that if you forget to issue a `stop()` anywhere at all, it would never be called (one issue with that being that you wouldn't know if your offsets got committed, another that a stale consumer might continue with auto-committing offsets that overwrite your newer consumer's offsets).

I had a go at trying to break reference cycles between the producer/consumer and their worker threads  at some point, and at the time that wasn't sufficient to deal with it (because there were further reference cycles). However, we've since cleaned up a few ref cycles (see #258, #262, and 583087bb25), and so I wonder if it would be doable now.

Would you be interested in looking into that as an alternative solution? There would be a benefit in that it would allow instances to stop as they go out of scope, rather than lingering (and continuing to auto-commit) until program exit.

I do think your implementation in this pullreq is very neat - also careful to remove registered callbacks during `stop()` - and it would perhaps be nice to bring it under `pykafka.utils` (that is, as an external facility, instead of as internal consumer/producer logic) for users who want to be absolutely certain that instances are stopped cleanly. (One reason not to make it internal to the producer/consumer is that the `atexit` handler holding a reference to `self` will always keep the reference count at at least 1 until program exit.)

Thanks!

It'd be great to leverage the existing `reset_offset_on_start` kwarg for this use case, but when the kwarg is set to `False`, the `fetch_offset()` method would be called [here](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/60cdff36524e221b458541864c61f6dadbb066f4/pykafka/simpleconsumer.py#L259). This may lead to offset reset attempts. 

My impression is that `reset_offset_on_start` is supposed to prevent resets on immediate start of a consumer, but subsequent resets patterns are not impacted. We might potentially lose this functionality if we use this param to prevent any `offset_reset` calls.

@stefanv I'm not sure what's unclear so here's a few clarifying potshots: =)

- imagine I have an array of grey values in the range [1e10, 5e10]. If you put 0 in the borders, that erosion is going to completely screw up downstream analysis.
- the right thing to do in the borders is application specific (hence the many options to `numpy.pad`), but a constant value is not often it and when it's not, it's one of the more dramatic ways to fail. (`reflect`, for example, will ensure pad data is in the same range as input data.)
- setting 0 as the pad value can result in weird artefacts when combining erosions and dilations, see eg #1309.
- in binary_erosion, if you set 1 as the boundary value, if the near-boundary value is 0, nothing happens; if it's e.g. 255, it gets replaced by 1. But at the end of the operation (or maybe at the start) everything gets converted to bool, so it doesn't matter.

Any other questions? =)

I don't think this would work either, though I'm less certain this time.

Django writes all the session data encoding in a single string as `session_data` in the `django_session` table. Even if the session_security's session data is not updated in the long running request, the rest of the session data will be. That data won't include the latest session_security data containing the user's activity and it will be re-encoded as a string back into the `django_session` table. The next request will decode this `session_data` and it won't include the latest session_security activity either.

However, this is all conjectures so maybe it'll behave differently?

Can you describe some specific failure scenarios where master is not responding correctly and failing over would be helpful to the situation? In the full disk example, in a typical deployment all nodes have the same amount of disk, so failing over is unlikely to improve availability. More likely you will then have a full disk on all nodes and if implemented correctly a cascade of failovers across all nodes, if not, a neverending cycle of failovers.

Partial hardware failures (I/O errors or slowness, etc.) could and I think should be monitored and handled outside of Patroni. Probably by removing the whole node from the cluster. To turn a node readonly an external agent could already turn on nofailover tag of Patroni and trigger a failover.

If you know what rows you want to insert at table creation time, I recommend using the [Client.load_table_from_file()](https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/latest/bigquery/reference.html#google.cloud.bigquery.client.Client.load_table_from_file) method to insert the rows using a StringIO object as the file.

For example:

```
from google.cloud.bigquery import LoadJobConfig
from six import StringIO

destination_table = self.client.dataset(dataset_id).table(table_id)
job_config = LoadJobConfig()
job_config.write_disposition = 'WRITE_APPEND'
job_config.source_format = 'NEWLINE_DELIMITED_JSON'
rows = []

for row in maybe_a_dataframe:
    row_json = row.to_json(force_ascii=False, date_unit='s', date_format='iso')
    rows.append(row_json)

body = StringIO('{}\n'.format('\n'.join(rows)))

self.client.load_table_from_file(
    body,
    destination_table,
    job_config=job_config).result()
```

The key takeaway: the API representation of bindings is really gross to interact with. Client side it would be much nicer if bindings were a dict with roles as keys, and members as values. So instead of code like:

```
roles = [binding['role'] for binding in policy.bindings]
if my_role in roles:
   members = policy.bindings[roles.index(my_role)]
   members.append(my_member)
else:
   policy.bindings.append({'role': my_role, 'members': [my_member]})
```

We had code like:
```
members = policy.bindings.setdefault(role, []).append(my_member)
```

The future only bubbles exceptions that occur with the actual API call, not with individual callbacks. You'll need to do your own error handling to capture those, something like:

```python
def callback(message):
    try:
        ...
     catch Exception as e:
         # set some flag/mutex/condition to signal the main thread.
```

Hi @americanwookie 

I think this looks great and we should merge it soon. I do however have a few nit picks that I wanted to ask you to take care of before commit. I have left those as inline comments.

Regarding your discussion about what the better interface is '1:00:00' or 3600 I can see both ends of that argument. I can follow @robwebset arguments that seconds is somewhat cleaner, on ther other hand, if implemented in a UI, it may very well be, that the have a integer control for each one of hours, minutes and seconds, which would make the string the better choice. Let's stick with int for how and see what happens.

There is one thing that I will comment on here. We, some time ago, abandoned the idea of return code (i.e. returning True or False), though not all the methods have been updated to reflect the change. Point is, that the policy that we are aiming for now is, that if the method executes, it is a success, otherwise an exception will be raised. I think this also opens up for some small refactoring to make the code a little cleaner.

Regarding your statement earlier about disabling a pylint warning. Just as a general observation. For Python we put readability at the top of the list of priorities, so variable names should be descriptive and we very rarely compromise on that. Also, and to follow up on readability, single use variables are not frowned upon, if they help readability (and besides, method or function local variables are very fast to create).

I've implemented logging so it's 100% configurable in django's settings.LOGGERS. Our default logging is the specified in our default deployment, i.e. `kolibri.deployments.default.settings.base`.

This includes two different log files: One for standard logging `~/.kolibri/kolibri.log` and one for debug logging: `~/.kolibri/debug.log`. At some point, we'll want to add a `--debug` flag for the kolibri command which can override these mechanisms through `kolibri.utils.conf.config` which is the configuration stack that's loaded before the Django settings module.

Changing the language codes could then break the content that uses 3-letter codes as the corresponding id would no longer exist. Also, certain languages have 3-letter codes because the 2-letter representation has been taken (e.g. Finnish and Filipino), so I'm not sure how cleanly certain languages will be translated to a new 2-letter scheme. Another one that may be useful here while I'm at it. Ensures a container dies with the program. 

Naturally this could be modified to support it's own kwargs (stuff like `should_rm` or `timeout`) that are stripped from the dictionary passed to start

```
import atexit

def safe_start(client, *args, **kwargs):
  def container_cleanup(client, cid):
    client.stop(cid)

  client.start(*args, **kwargs)
  if len(args) == 1:
    atexit.register(container_cleanup, client, args[0])
  else:
    atexit.register(container_cleanup, client, kwargs['container'])
```

```
b'\x01\xf8{\x05\xa9odash/v1.0.0-stable/windows-amd64/go1.8.3\xcc\xc5\x83eth?\xc5\x83eth>\x80\xb8@.\x1bb\x00s>\xb77W\xb6\x1bhK{V\x865\x8b\x0e\x15\x86\x90\xeb)\x90-\xee\xd0]\x95<\xac\xe2 \xc4\xf9\xf3\x01\xb4\x8a\xee\xdc\xea\x90b\xc4\t\xc9d\x18S$\xbb\xc9\xa1gI\x1clY\xe0\x94\xc4\xf9'
```

The first byte is the command id. In this case it is 0x01, which is the `Disconnect` command, but the payload (see below) matches exactly that of the `Hello` command (see https://github.com/ethereum/wiki/wiki/%C3%90%CE%9EVp2p-Wire-Protocol), which suggests a broken client implementation.

```
>>> rlp.decode(d[1:])
[b'\x05', b'odash/v1.0.0-stable/windows-amd64/go1.8.3', [[b'eth', b'?'], [b'eth', b'>']], b'', b'.\x1bb\x00s>\xb77W\xb6\x1bhK{V\x865\x8b\x0e\x15\x86\x90\xeb)\x90-\xee\xd0]\x95<\xac\xe2 \xc4\xf9\xf3\x01\xb4\x8a\xee\xdc\xea\x90b\xc4\t\xc9d\x18S$\xbb\xc9\xa1gI\x1clY\xe0\x94\xc4\xf9']
```

And we're not the first ones to notice that: https://github.com/ethereum/pyethapp/issues/288

I think the best alternative here is to catch that exception in `Disconnect.decode()` and log a warning

Some thoughts on what this API might look like.

For cases where we just want to loop over the peers once.

```python
for header in batch:
    peer_query = BlockHeadersPeerQuery(min_td=target_td)
    async for peer in peer_pool.filter_peers_for(peer_query):
        response = await peer.requests.get_block_headers(headers)
```

The pattern above has a deficiency which I think we can fix.  Since we don't always get all the things back that we request, we pretty much always have to implement a pattern that aggregates the *missing* elements and puts them back into the queue to be re-requested from another peer.

What I'd like to figure out is how to capture this concept in our API so that it's handled in a natural way, reducing the number of nested loops, etc.  In order to do this, I'm imagining a streaming API that will continue to yield peers indefinitely.

```python
for headers in batch:
    with peer_pool.stream_peers_for(BlockHeadersPeerQuery(min_td=target_td)) as stream:
        while True:
            peer = stream.next(headers)  # tells the generator what we are requesting
            response = peer.requests.get_block_headers(headers)
            missing = ...  # compute which things the peer didn't return us.
            stream.add_missing_to_peer(peer, missing)
````

Here's a few things that warrant explaining.

- `stream.next(headers)` would inform the generator which headers we are wanting to request so that it could provide us with an appropriate peer.
- `stream.add_missing_to_peer(peer, missing)` updates some tracking data somewhere which is keeping track of which peers do not have certain data.

Ideally the underlying API would also prioritize the fastest peers as measured by some combination of metrics that are collected for the given command.

Other findings for transfer/generation:

* ACE seems to count the number of brackets. `[ [ ]\n` does not trigger processing, but the next `]\n` does.
* ACE does not consider escaped brackets. `[ \]` and `[ \\]` both trigger processing.
* Multiple MRSs on one line are possible; `[ ] [ ]` triggers processing for two inputs (there are two sets of outputs).

I think for `AceTransferer` and `AceGenerator`, the `interact()` method should just raise an exception if the input is not a simple MRS (based on simple bracket matching rather than actual MRS parsing)

#!/bin/sh
_CONDA_ROOT="/Users/sherbein/opt/miniconda2"
#!/bin/sh

_conda_set_vars() {
    # set _CONDA_SHELL_FLAVOR
    if [ -n "${BASH_VERSION:+x}" ]; then
        _CONDA_SHELL_FLAVOR=bash
    elif [ -n "${ZSH_VERSION:+x}" ]; then
        _CONDA_SHELL_FLAVOR=zsh
    elif [ -n "${KSH_VERSION:+x}" ]; then
        _CONDA_SHELL_FLAVOR=ksh
    elif [ -n "${POSH_VERSION:+x}" ]; then
        _CONDA_SHELL_FLAVOR=posh
    else
        # https://unix.stackexchange.com/a/120138/92065
        local _q="$(ps -p$$ -o cmd="",comm="",fname="" 2>/dev/null | sed 's/^-//' | grep -oE '\w+' | head -n1)"
        if [ "$_q" = dash ]; then
            _CONDA_SHELL_FLAVOR=dash
        else
            (>&2 echo "Unrecognized shell.")
            return 1
        fi
    fi

    # https://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/4650/determining-path-to-sourced-shell-script/
    local script_dir
    case "$_CONDA_SHELL_FLAVOR" in
        bash) script_dir="$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")";;
        zsh) script_dir="$(dirname "${(%):-%x}")";;  # http://stackoverflow.com/a/28336473/2127762
        dash) x=$(lsof -p $$ -Fn0 | tail -1); script_dir="$(dirname "${x#n}")";;
        *) script_dir="$(cd "$(dirname "$_")" && echo "$PWD")";;
    esac

    if [ -z "${_CONDA_ROOT+x}" ]; then
        _CONDA_ROOT="$(dirname "$script_dir")"
    fi

}

_conda_script_is_sourced() {
    # http://stackoverflow.com/a/28776166/2127762
    sourced=0
    if [ -n "${ZSH_EVAL_CONTEXT:+x}" ]; then
      case $ZSH_EVAL_CONTEXT in *:file) sourced=1;; esac
    elif [ -n "${KSH_VERSION:+x}" ]; then
      [ "$(cd $(dirname -- $0) && pwd -P)/$(basename -- $0)" != "$(cd $(dirname -- ${.sh.file}) && pwd -P)/$(basename -- ${.sh.file})" ] && sourced=1
    elif [ -n "${BASH_VERSION:+x}" ]; then
      [ "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}" = "$0" ] && sourced=1
    else # All other shells: examine $0 for known shell binary filenames
      # Detects `sh` and `dash`; add additional shell filenames as needed.
      case ${0##*/} in sh|dash) sourced=0;; *) sourced=1;; esac
    fi
    return $sourced
}

if ! _conda_script_is_sourced; then
    (
      >&2 echo "Error: activate must be sourced. Run 'source activate envname'"
      >&2 echo "instead of 'activate envname'."
    )
    exit 1
fi

_conda_set_vars
. "$_CONDA_ROOT/etc/profile.d/conda.sh" || return $?
_conda_activate "$@"

According to fetch.py, fetch_repodata will run in parallel if it can import concurrent.futures. 

If this download run in serial, it is not hard to add progress bar using existing library. However, when run in parallel, we may have to modify progress bar library to support this.

The reason there is no 'nomkl' for gensim, is that gensim does not link to
MKL (I'm not sure if it could).
There are only a few packages with the nomkl feature: numpy, scipy,
scikit-learn, numexpr
Most packages don't have a 'nomkl' feature, because they cannot be linked
to MKL, such as: python, pandas, openssl, conda, pycosat, bokeh, pyyaml,
freetype, ... <hundreds more>....

To make the watchdog code async and independent of timescale - use explicit time unit in the wait statement.


Below is a piece of code that we've in Go2OVM lib doing similar thing - in this case it waits for a signal or a predefined time and exits 

You may want to try that.

Then user can supply arg in multiples of NS.

'''verilog

`define GO2OVM_WAIT(END_SIG, WDOG_VAL = GO2OVM_WDOG_DEL_IN_NS) \
fork \
begin \
string msg; \
wait (END_SIG); \
msg = $sformatf ("Wait seen in condition: %s ", `GO2OVM_DISP_ARG(END_SIG) ); \
`g2o_display (msg); \
end \
begin \
string msg; \
#(WDOG_VAL * 1ns); \
msg = $sformatf ("WDOG expired after waiting for: %0d %s %s", WDOG_VAL, "ns Wait condition: ", `GO2OVM_DISP_ARG(END_SIG) ); \
`g2o_error (msg); \
end \
join_any \
disable fork; 

'''

Ok got it working, it was our fault, the ticket can be closed

The problem was in the setup of the admin panel. 
Basically here is an instruction:

- Go to Azure **Cloud** portal 
- Create an App in AD -> Register App
- Grant Permissions to "Azure Stack" (_Note: Azure Stack permissions are not listed, you have to specifically search it - not user-friendly to be honest_)
- Create a Secret Key for an App
- Go to Azure **Stack** portal
- Go to your Resource group and select required (or do for all of them)
- in the selected resource group AMI permissions (where you have all users from AD) specifically search for your App name
- Add it as an owner of RG

So our problem was that we didn't grant in Azure Stack AMI permission to the App to manage resource group.

Well if two server extensions changed the template path... who wins? if they both overload `notebook.html`, they can't know which one will actually resolve. It will probably stable-ish, for a given user, but still pretty non-deterministic.

If your model expects BGR, then you should pass BGR images to the attack (which is what you seem to be doing by changing RGB images to BGR images using `image[:,:,::-1]`). Obviously, Foolbox has no knowledge whether the model expects RGB and you pass RGB, or whether the model expects BGR and you pass BGR. Accordingly, the adversarial that is returned by the attack will have the same format as the image that is passed to the attack, so in your case, `image[:,:,::-1]` and `adversarial` have the same color channel ordering (e.g. BGR in your case). So to get RGB adversarials, you need to reverse the channel order again.

@mingyr Hi, thanks for your detailed comment.

I've looked into this before and unfortunately there are no publicly exposed APIs from TF which allow us to have more control over this. I would like to be able to capture the surrounding device context when constructing a module, as your middle example shows. However unlike the various kinds of scopes, `tf.device` doesn't return anything you can hang onto and reenter, eg:

```
with tf.variable_scope('some_name') as scope:
  print(scope)  # Prints a VariableScope object we can query, reenter, etc

with tf.device('/cpu:0') as device_information:
  print(device_information)  # prints `None`
```

Without being able to capture the context, we cannot respect device placement directives around where we construct modules. If you read the TF source you see that this works by accessing various private members of the `Graph` object and others, and it would be a huge maintenance burden for us to start relying on these private APIs.

We've had some conversations with the TF team about creating an external more advanced use of device placement (and other things(), and those discussions are ongoing. I hope to have some improvement in this area in the public version soon.

It's very useful to hear from users about the pain points of the library, so once again thankyou.

Seems like this change won't be so trivial.
`read()` was not designed to work with base paths, but with specific entity instances, e.g. `Product().read()` will try to send GET request to `/katello/api/products/:id`, not to base `/katello/api/products`.
On the contrary `search()` is designed to send GET to base path, that's probably why it was chosen for Ping() entity.

Switching to EntityReadMixin will introduce at least 2 issues:
1. When sending GET request, entity instance path will be chosen instead of base path. This issue can be workarounded with custom `read_raw()` method like this:
```python
    def read_raw(self, params=None):
        return client.get(
            self.path('base'),
            params=params,
            **self._server_config.get_client_kwargs()
        )
```
2. When processing response, nailgun will expect `id` of entity to be present in outputs and will rise exception as it won't be found. `id` is set in entity's `__init__` method, which makes it a bit tricky to override. My best guess right now is to override `get_fields()` with smth like that:
```python
    def get_fields(self):
        fields = self._fields.copy()
        fields.pop('id')
        return fields
```

But introducing 2 such workarounds makes it debatable which approach is better - to use `search()` instead of `read()` or to use `read()` but with 2 workarounds. Thoughts?

One other design question I have for this issue is whether or not there's utility to making the `supported_versions` be a class vs. instance variable of the concrete_subclasses.

As we now understand class attributes are accessible in the `LazyAttributesMixin` since that method has references to the actual classes.

Some reasons we might prefer a class attribute over an instance attribute:

   1.   It's a closer mapping to system we're modeling.   Specifically there is 1 class (type) per terminal URI, all instances of that class, should have the same `supported_versions` in other words, I think `supported_versions` is a class attributes, not an instance attribute.
   2.   Instantiating and object...   just to see if we should really be instantiating it...   feels hacky.   Or more technically, is risky, particularly if `__init__` has extra-object side effects.    Mostly we've avoided that kind of side-effect (anti-pattern), but we decided to add exactly that kind of side effect in this very feature (and I think it's a good idea!), because we now have a communication between client and device during `ManagementRoot` instantiation!

  To be clear, I'm not saying it's clearly the case that we should do this, but now is probably the right time to spend a minute thinking about trade-offs.

Before I submit a pull request, I want to point out that the error message given for the original example appears to be misleading.

>ValueError: RRULE UNTIL values must be specified in UTC when DTSTART is timezone-aware

In the original example, the time zone of UNTIL is actually specified in UTC.

`rule = rrule.rrulestr("RRULE:FREQ=WEEKLY;WKST=MO;BYDAY=MO,TU;UNTIL=20180331T200000Z")`

I believe the original example is failing for a different reason entirely, which is the following sentence in RFC 5545 Section 3.3.10.

>Furthermore, if the"DTSTART" property is specified as a date with local time, then the UNTIL rule part MUST also be specified as a date with localtime.

That condition is enforced by the line 452 in rrule.py.

`if (self._dtstart.tzinfo is not None) != (self._until.tzinfo is not None):`

In fact, it appears the condition described in the error message is not properly enforced. In the version of the module without the changes I made, I can successfully create the rule:

`>>> rule = rrule.rrulestr("""DTSTART:20180306T053600-05`
`... RRULE:FREQ=WEEKLY;WKST=MO;BYDAY=MO,TU;UNTIL=20180331T200000-05`
`... """)`

Here, DTSTART is timezone aware but UNTIL is not in UTC.

Hmm, you can't use `verify` in each mock fixture (see comment above) because it would get called more than once if a test used more than one mock. 

This seems to work as an equivalent of `@test` around every function:

    @fixture(scope="function", autouse=True)
    def mocks():
        """
        Makes sure any mocks are reset before and verified after the test.
        """
        clear_expectations()
        clear_calls()
        yield
        verify()

You can't use `@test` itself because fixtures get created before the test function gets called, so when the test is called the decorator resets all the Fakes that were created as fixtures. :(

Another (perhaps unimportant) reason why I'm pessimistic towards the use of `DISTINCT` is that it's really expensive. 
Look at these two queries:
```
explain analyze
select id, as_epoch(last_modified) AS last_modified,
MD5(jsonb_pretty(data)) AS data
from records
WHERE parent_id LIKE '/buckets/build-hub/collections/%'
ORDER BY last_modified DESC
LIMIT 10;

explain analyze
select DISTINCT id, as_epoch(last_modified) AS last_modified,
MD5(jsonb_pretty(data)) AS data
from records
WHERE parent_id LIKE '/buckets/build-hub/collections/%'
ORDER BY last_modified DESC
LIMIT 10;
```

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Limit  (cost=0.42..4.75 rows=10 width=87) (actual time=0.064..0.170 rows=10 loops=1)

               Sort Key: (as_epoch(last_modified)) DESC, id COLLATE "C", (md5(jsonb_pretty(data)))
               Sort Method: external merge  Disk: 18352kB
               ->  Seq Scan on records  (cost=0.00..76337.24 rows=183242 width=87) (actual time=0.067..1699.084 rows=183241 loops=1)

>The cache wouldn't know if the match was over, so if you request the currentmatch right before the match actually ends, it will end up caching it for 30min, then the player could get into another match 5 minutes afterwards, thus any subsequent calls to currentmatch would be incorrect.

This idea of "requesting an object just before it changes means that it's cached after it has changed" is true of every data type, not just `CurrentMatch`. We have to make decisions about what is reasonable behavior, and have to balance reasonable caching times with incorrect/current data. As you mentioned, you can change the defaults if you don't like them, that's why we made them configurable from the settings.

You can't just pass in a partial settings dict. You need to define the entire settings, not just a small part of it.

`cass.apply_settings` doesn't take a filename, it takes a dictionary.

You can use `cass.get_default_config` to get a copy of the default config, and modify the dictionary before passing it into `cass.apply_settings`.

Hello @pavloblindnology ,
Librealsense use of the disparity term is consistent with the standard definition for stereo vision:  [Disparity(displacement)=focal_length*stereo_baseline/Z](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_stereo_vision)

Note that DISPARITY_16 is a legacy format and is not natively supported in librealsense2 , i.e. you cannot request and receive frames of this type.
The current SDK v2 does use a related DISPARITY_32 format internally, though the usage is limited to post-processing blocks to obtain uniform data filtering.

A sizeable number of SSH configuration options are available. Here's one of the more interesting items in my ~/.ssh/config` file:

```
Host *.example.com,!collab.example.com
    User root
    StrictHostKeyChecking no
    UserKnownHostsFile /dev/null
```

This tells the OpenSSH client to log in as root, to automatically accept new host keys, and to send those new host keys to `/dev/null`, and to do this for all systems that have a hostname of `*.example.com` except `collab.example.com`. Trying to build that kind of SSH client configuration logic in to Pulp Smash would be a _hellish_ mistake.

---

I spent some time toying around with paramiko, and it seems like a reasonably nice solution. There is one issue that'll be bothersome: it has terrible key handling logic. I kept getting errors like this:

> paramiko.ssh_exception.SSHException: Server 'pine.ichimonji10.name' not found in known_hosts

That error was due to my ecdsa-sha2-nistp256 key not being properly handled. We can work with that, though, by using a subset of possible ECDSA key types and RSA keys.

---

Here's a rough and working example of how to use paramiko. I don't have time to add in support for ssh-agent right now (need to head out for an appointment), but it should get us started.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
import paramiko


def main():
    paramiko.util.log_to_file('/home/ichimonji10/tmp/paramiko.log')

    # Read values from config file instead of hard-coding them into script.
    config = paramiko.SSHConfig()
    config.parse(open('/home/ichimonji10/.ssh/config'))

    # Paramio refuses to connect to unknown hosts.
    client = paramiko.SSHClient()
    client.load_host_keys('/home/ichimonji10/.ssh/known_hosts')

    # When one reads the configuration file, values are lowercased. They are not
    # renamed for use by connect().
    kwargs = config.lookup('github.com')
    if 'user' in kwargs:  # defaults to the current local username
        kwargs['username'] = kwargs.pop('user')
    if 'identityfile' in kwargs:
        kwargs['key_filename'] = kwargs.pop('identityfile')

    # Connect and disconnect.
    client.connect(**kwargs)
    client.close()


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

Attached is a version of ACLMiddleware I'm experimenting with.

Note that my implementation has:
* `get_principals` returns principals (i.e. `['group:Admin', 'user:1234']`)
* `permits` is a function that returns True or False with arguments (`resource` typically an instance of the model, `principals` as above, `permission` such as 'edit').

Those implementation details aren't Django specific, but I think this problem is more general than the Django, and this is where the conversation is happening.

As for constraints from previous comments:
1) this doesn't support permissions per field (though `Meta` could pretty easily be extended to support that),
2) when querying `node`, we don't (can't?) get access to the `ObjectType` so we have to re-implement the `get_node` method on ObjectType classes.

```python
class ACLMiddleware:
    def resolve(self, next, root, args, context, info):
        result = next(root, args, context, info)
        graphene_type = getattr(info.return_type, 'graphene_type', None)
        if not isinstance(graphene_type, SQLAlchemyObjectTypeMeta):
            return result

        permission = getattr(graphene_type._meta, 'permission', None)
        if not permission:
            return result

        authenticated_user = context.get('authenticated_user', None)
        principals = _User.get_principals(
            context['session'],
            authenticated_user,
        )
        return result if permits(result.value, principals, permission) else None
```

The `metadata_path` property within projector_config.pbtxt must be relative to the log directory (logdir). The logdir in this case is named `processed`. Because projector_config.pbtxt looks like this: ``` embeddings { tensor_name: "embedding:0" metadata_path: "processed/vocab_1000.tsv" } ``` , the projector plugin attempts to load `processed/processed/vocab_1000.tsv`, which does not exist. If you set `metadata_path` to "vocab_1000.tsv", you should be able to successfully load the labels, as I did. Screenshot: TensorBoard (the embedding projector frontend) misleadingly halts at the "Parsing metadata" step because it attempts to parse the metadata string of `"/home/usr/agent007/Desktop/tf-stanford-tutorials/examples/processed/processed/vocab_1000.tsv" not found, or is not a file`, which is obviously not valid metadata content - it's just an error message retrieved from the server. All in all, * You can solve this problem by excluding the logdir from `metadata_path` * The projector frontend needs to gracefully handle the case in which the tsv file containing metadata cannot be found (instead of misleadingly erring during the parsing stage). I'll create a PR soon that adds a nice message to the embedding projector.

Internally, `scikit-learn` appears to just be breaking the data into pieces, which it feeds into [`_sparse_encode`]( https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/0.19.1/sklearn/decomposition/dict_learning.py#L297-L308 ). Since the splitting is done just to make the jobs [roughly even]( https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/0.19.1/sklearn/decomposition/dict_learning.py#L295 ), it would certainly be reasonable to run the `SparseCoder` on existing chunks of a Dask Array that is being fit to as long as [the whole dictionary]( https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/0.19.1/sklearn/decomposition/dict_learning.py#L299 ) is provided. This would then use whatever parallelism Dask is setup to use.

We can bypass the job submission within `sparse_encode` by setting [`n_jobs == 1`]( https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/0.19.1/sklearn/decomposition/dict_learning.py#L282 ). There are some [initial checks]( https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/0.19.1/sklearn/decomposition/dict_learning.py#L255-L261 ) we can bypass as long as we do them ourselves. Also there are [some initial computations on the data]( https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/0.19.1/sklearn/decomposition/dict_learning.py#L266-L280 ) that we can compute once and scatter to the workers to avoid repetition. This should allow us to run just [the relevant function in parallel]( https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/0.19.1/sklearn/decomposition/dict_learning.py#L283-L290 ) with only a little reworking of [`SparseCodingMixin.transform`]( https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/0.19.1/sklearn/decomposition/dict_learning.py#L806 ).

**pyscript** is a command built into the cmd2 framework which allows you to run Python scripts within a cmd2 application.  These scripts can run custom cmd2 commands and be used to automate conditional control flow.

See the **python_scripting.py** example cmd2 application:
https://github.com/python-cmd2/cmd2/blob/master/examples/python_scripting.py

And look at the **conditional.py** example Python script for use with it via the **pyscript** command:
https://github.com/python-cmd2/cmd2/blob/master/examples/scripts/conditional.py

So you could run the python_scripting.py cmd2 application like so:

```Bash
cd examples
python3 python_scripting.py
```
Then within it you can run the conditional.py script like so:

```Bash
'.../cmd2/examples' $ pyscript scripts/conditional.py ~
```
where "~" is an argument passed to the script which is either a relative or absolute path to a directory.

Unfortunately the `TLS-SNI` challenge type has been disabled permanently by Let's Encrypt because of concerns and demonstrated exploits against shared hosting provider platform implementations, see: https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/important-what-you-need-to-know-about-tls-sni-validation-issues/50811

The supported challenge types are `dns` and `http`. 

I'd believe `sudo certbot certonly --standalone --preferred-challenges http -d my.domain.com` would work for you, if you just make sure port 80 is free and not blocked by the firewall.

`--debug-challenges` currently cannot be used with `certbot renew`. The reason for this is `certbot renew` is usually run automatically from something like `cron` and we didn't want Certbot to prompt the user and hang indefinitely. With that said, this feature would be valuable and we could potentially make it work with `renew` by including `--force-interactive` on the command line which was suggested in #4925.

In the meantime, you should be able to use `--debug-challenges` by running the following command instead:
```
certbot certonly --cert-name linestarve.com --debug-challenges
```

For the first problem, sos sets the template according to `walltime`, but sos does not enforce walltime. (It has a queue option `max_walltime` but that has a different usage). The problem here is that cluster has different strategies in handling walltime (grace period) and it can be confusing if sos kills the job before PBS.

sos should detect the case when the job is terminated by PBS, because then the pulse thread will also be killed and sos should detect the case and mark it as canceled. I will see if this is still the case.

Hi there,

Thanks for the interest. Multiple identical hosts can be passed in host list, but yes currently they can only use the same port. HostName as per `.ssh/config` does not help either because that is also per-host. It allows you to have multiple identical hostname entries, but only one will be used. For the paramiko client, `.ssh/config` is used already but has the above limitation.

Perhaps the best solution would be to allow `(host, port)` tuples as entries in host list. So the following becomes possible:

```
hosts = [('localhost', 22), ('localhost', 2222), <..>]
client = ParallelSSHClient(hosts)
<..>
```

That is a simple enough change to make for next release. PRs welcome of course.

"visible"

""

> we might want to distinguish between the different cancel scopes in the stack (e.g. the first time we hit cancel point X, cancel the topmost scope; the second time we hit cancel point X, cancel the next-to-topmost scope, etc., and only move on to the next cancel point after we've exercised all the scopes at the first cancel point)

Not sure what I was thinking here exactly, but it doesn't make sense to pick random internal cancel scopes and cancel them, since cancel scopes don't spontaneously cancel themselves like that normally. The obvious case is testing external cancellation of some public API, like:
```python
cancel_tester = CancelTester()
while cancel_tester.work_to_do():
    with open_cancel_scope() as scope:
        cancel_tester.set_scope(scope)
        await do_http_request()
    # make some assertions about how it handled it, check for ResourceWarnings, whatever
```
(This is pretty awkward way of doing the API, ergonomics could be improved, but you get the idea.)

The other case that might make sense is for testing internal timeouts, since timeouts can go off semi-randomly. For this we'd want to integrate with MockClock I suppose, like -- check when the next timeout could happen and make time jump forward to that.

> If a merged PR becomes to mean "a change worth releasing", does that mean that every single PR is supposed to improve the overall quality of the project?

I mean... PRs are always supposed to improve the overall quality of the project, right? :-)

I believe that in Hypothesis's setup, they distinguish between two kinds of PRs: trivial ones that don't have any news entry and don't trigger a release, and non-trivial ones that have a news entry and do trigger a release. So that means that e.g. a commit that just tweaks `.travis.yml` or fixes a `README` typo won't necessarily generate a release. But it does mean that after every PR, master has to be release**able**, because every time a non-trivial PR comes along its release will roll-up and include all the recent trivial PRs.

Now, as a general principle, I think this is what we want to strive for anyway. If a PR is known to cause a significant performance regression, then the options are (1) decide the change is important enough to justify the regression, (2) block merging that PR until the regression is fixed, (3) merge the PR anyway, and then block releasing until the regression is fixed. The main difference between (2) and (3) is that in (2), this *one* change is blocked from reaching users, while in (3), *all* changes are blocked from reaching users. Plus it's just awkward and frustrating to try to keep track of which known issues are currently lurking in master, and if you have a steady stream of PRs like this then you can even end up in a state where master is never releasable; this is how you end up with the cliche of an open-source project that makes like, one release every 5 years or something. So in theory, we should prefer (1) or (2) and avoid (3) entirely.

But I've known all this theory for like a decade, and yet 0.2.0 still happened – I think because the new features were large and complicated enough that I needed multiple PRs worth of iterations to figure them all out. There is a way around this though: make a branch, submit multiple PRs against that branch, and then once the branch is in a nice complete state, merge it into master.

There's also another case, where a PR causes a significant performance regression but we don't realize it until after it's merged. In this case I think it's fine if we let the release out – if we didn't catch it before merging then it's not likely that letting it sit in master for a few weeks, with no-one using it, is going to help us catch it either. The sooner we release it, the sooner someone will notice the problem, file a bug, and then we can fix it and improve our CI so that the next time this happens we can catch it in the PR stage.

> Re: formatting, I'd leave this alone in Trio for now - not least because Hypothesis will probably try using Black soon. Basically we fail CI if formatting again produces a git diff. It's possible to have Travis commit the diff and try to push it back to the user branch if they accept maintainer pushes (the default), but this risks nasty problems for inexperienced users of Git and IMO isn't worth it.

Oh, we've been using yapf in this mode since before black existed :-). The musing in my original post was about whether we can make things even simpler for contributors.

> I don't like revision histories that are cluttered with formatting changes.

All else being equal, I don't either. But the number 1 priority for a project that wants to successful and attract contributors is to make contributions as easy and frictionless as possible (esp. early contributions) – [see e.g.](https://twitter.com/brainwane/status/988794500041457665) and surrounding thread. Slightly messy revision histories have much less effect on project success, so I don't worry about them as much :-).

> I need a set of tasks associated with each connection to a listener

Ah, right, then I think a very natural approach would be something like
```python
async def connection_handler(sock):
    with sock:
        async with trio.open_nursery as nursery:
            nursery.spawn(first_worker, nursery)  # spawns more tasks into this nursery as needed
```

> Do nurseries have much overhead?

Not really. And if it becomes a problem I'd rather work on cutting down the overhead than try to create something that's almost-the-same but different :-)

> A disadvantage of the nursery approach is that it gives up control of shutdown order.

Hmm, interesting point. I made the conscious decision to initially support only "group" cancellation with cancel scopes to keep things simple internally and keep cancellation and tasks more orthogonal, but I can see how targeting individual tasks might be useful for e.g. [rest-for-one supervisors](http://learnyousomeerlang.com/supervisors). Plus whatever your mysterious use case is :-)

> Behind the scenes how does the nursery end the tasks? Does it inject a cancel or just abandon the tasks?

Eek, yes, it cancels them. Trio never ever ever abandons tasks, that's like the number one rule that the entire rest of the design is warped to fit :-).

> I keep looking for a `task.cancel()` or `nursery.task_cancel(task)` method without any luck :-)

As alluded to above, there is none (currently) – just `nursery.cancel_scope.cancel()` which cancels everything inside the nursery. But I'm extremely interested to hear more about what you're trying to do.
